Name: latent_smoothness_regularization
Title: Latent Smoothness Regularization: Training-Free Inference Optimization via Spectral Stability
Short Hypothesis: By enforcing smoothness in the latent space during inference via spectral regularization of activation dynamics, we can reduce computational overhead while maintaining or improving model performance, particularly under distribution shifts.
Related Work: Existing approaches to inference optimization often rely on static profiling or costly calibration, failing to adapt to dynamic input distributions. Recent work like Spectral Quantization Routing (SQR) leverages activation geometry for mixed-precision inference but does not explicitly address latent smoothness. Our proposal uniquely focuses on spectral regularization of latent dynamics to enforce smoothness, which we hypothesize will lead to more stable and efficient inference without additional training.
Abstract: We propose Latent Smoothness Regularization (LSR), a training-free framework that optimizes inference efficiency by enforcing smoothness in the latent space through spectral regularization of activation dynamics. By leveraging the spectral properties of hidden states, LSR dynamically adjusts computational pathways to reduce overhead while maintaining performance. We hypothesize that smooth latent representations lead to more stable and efficient inference, particularly under distribution shifts. Our approach computes spectral diagnostics on-the-fly using randomized SVD, mapping them to a regularization policy that enforces strict latency budgets. We theoretically ground the regularization mechanism with PAC-Bayes bounds linking spectral smoothness to generalization stability. Evaluated on single-GPU setups across WILDS benchmarks, LSR achieves a superior throughput-accuracy Pareto frontier compared to static and heuristic baselines. This work bridges mechanistic interpretability with system-scale efficiency, offering a zero-cost, mathematically transparent method to enhance model reliability and optimize resource deployment in constrained environments.
Experiments: Evaluate LSR on WILDS benchmarks to measure accuracy retention and inference efficiency under distribution shifts.
Compare LSR to static and heuristic baselines in terms of throughput-accuracy Pareto frontiers.
Conduct ablation studies to isolate the impact of spectral regularization on latent smoothness and inference stability.
Measure the computational overhead of on-the-fly spectral diagnostics using randomized SVD.
Analyze the theoretical bounds linking spectral smoothness to PAC-Bayes generalization under perturbation.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The effectiveness of LSR may vary across different model architectures and tasks.
On-the-fly spectral diagnostics could introduce additional computational overhead, potentially offsetting gains in efficiency.
The proposed method assumes that latent smoothness correlates with inference stability, which may not hold in all cases.
Theoretical bounds may be difficult to establish rigorously, limiting the generalizability of the approach.
